skill.name: cc-skill-coding-standards
skill.description: Universal coding standards, best practices, and patterns for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development.
skill.summary: Universal coding standards and best practices for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: All 73 static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns (backtick execution, weak crypto, system reconnaissance) are Markdown code blocks and documentation examples, not executable code. This is a pure documentation skill with no executable functionality. No security risks identified.

low_findings.title: Documentation Code Examples Trigger Static Scanner
low_findings.description: The static scanner detected 53 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns. These are Markdown code blocks (```typescript) used for documentation, not actual shell execution. The skill contains only code examples and best practices documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear evidence these are Markdown code fences for TypeScript examples, not executable code. The pattern \`\`\`typescript appears throughout the file as documentation formatting.

low_findings.title: Fetch API Examples in Documentation
low_findings.description: Detected 2 instances of fetch() calls at lines 92 and 107. These are documentation examples showing proper async/await error handling patterns, not actual network requests made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls appear within TypeScript code blocks marked as '✅ GOOD' and '❌ BAD' examples for educational purposes. These are not executable code.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import for React Lazy Loading Example
low_findings.description: Detected dynamic import() at line 409. This is a documentation example showing React's lazy() pattern for code splitting, not actual dynamic imports executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import is inside a code block demonstrating React lazy loading best practices. No actual dynamic imports are executed by the skill itself.

content.user_title: Enforce Coding Standards Across Your Codebase
content.value_statement: Inconsistent code quality leads to bugs and technical debt. This skill provides universal coding standards, best practices, and patterns for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development to help you write clean, maintainable code.
actual_capabilities: Apply universal coding standards across TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js projects
Enforce naming conventions for variables, functions, and components
Implement immutability patterns and proper error handling
Review code against best practices for React hooks and state management
Validate API design standards and REST conventions
Check file organization and project structure compliance
limitations: Cannot automatically refactor existing code - provides guidance only
Does not run static analysis or linting tools directly
Standards are opinionated and may differ from your team's conventions
Does not replace comprehensive code review processes

use_cases.title: Code Review Assistant
use_cases.description: Use this skill during code reviews to validate that pull requests follow established coding standards and best practices.

use_cases.title: Onboarding New Developers
use_cases.description: Help new team members understand your codebase standards and conventions by providing consistent examples and patterns.

use_cases.title: Code Quality Improvement
use_cases.description: Apply these standards when refactoring legacy code to improve maintainability and reduce technical debt.

prompt_templates.title: Review Code Against Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code against the coding standards and best practices defined in the cc-skill-coding-standards skill. Identify any violations and suggest improvements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use during code review to check compliance with standards

prompt_templates.title: Refactor for Best Practices
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code to follow TypeScript and React best practices from the cc-skill-coding-standards skill. Focus on immutability, error handling, and component structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when improving code quality in existing code

prompt_templates.title: Generate Standards-Compliant Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a [component/function/module] that follows the coding standards from cc-skill-coding-standards. Include proper types, error handling, and documentation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when creating new code to ensure standards compliance

prompt_templates.title: Explain Standards Violations
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain why this code violates the coding standards in cc-skill-coding-standards. Reference specific principles like KISS, DRY, or YAGNI.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use to educate team members on why certain patterns matter

output_examples.input: Review this function for coding standards compliance: function get(u) { return fetch(u).then(d=>d.json()) }
output_examples.output: This function violates multiple standards: unclear naming (get, u, d), no error handling, no type safety, and returns data without checking response.ok. Refactored version includes: descriptive name (fetchMarketData), proper TypeScript types, comprehensive error handling with try/catch, and response validation.

output_examples.input: Generate a React button component following the standards
output_examples.output: Functional component with TypeScript interface (ButtonProps), proper prop types (children, onClick, disabled, variant), default values for optional props, and semantic HTML structure. Includes prop spreading and className composition.

output_examples.input: Check if this code follows immutability patterns: user.name = 'New Name'; items.push(newItem);
output_examples.output: This code violates the immutability principle. Direct mutation of objects and arrays causes unexpected side effects. Correct approach: use spread operator for objects ({...user, name: 'New Name'}) and arrays ([...items, newItem]) to create new references instead of mutating existing ones.

best_practices: Always use descriptive variable and function names that explain intent rather than abbreviated forms
Apply immutability patterns consistently using spread operators instead of direct mutation
Implement comprehensive error handling for async operations with try/catch blocks and response validation
Use TypeScript types and interfaces instead of 'any' to maintain type safety
anti_patterns: Avoid mutation of objects and arrays directly - use spread operators or immutable update patterns
Do not use 'any' type in TypeScript - always define proper interfaces and type annotations
Never omit error handling in async functions - always include try/catch with proper error messages
Avoid deeply nested conditionals - use early returns and guard clauses for clarity

faq.question: Are these coding standards mandatory for all projects?
faq.answer: No, these are recommended best practices. Adapt them to your team's specific needs and existing conventions. The standards provide a foundation for consistent, high-quality code.

faq.question: Can I use these standards with languages other than TypeScript and JavaScript?
faq.answer: While many principles like KISS, DRY, and YAGNI apply universally, the specific examples target TypeScript and JavaScript ecosystems. Adapt the concepts to your language of choice.

faq.question: How do I enforce these standards in my team?
faq.answer: Combine these standards with linting tools (ESLint, Prettier), pre-commit hooks, and code review checklists. Use the skill during reviews to catch violations early.

faq.question: What if the standards conflict with my existing codebase?
faq.answer: Apply standards incrementally. Prioritize new code and high-risk areas when refactoring. Gradual adoption is better than attempting a complete rewrite.

faq.question: Do these standards replace the need for code reviews?
faq.answer: No, these standards complement code reviews. They provide objective criteria for evaluation, but reviews also capture context-specific decisions and architectural considerations.

faq.question: How often should I update these standards?
faq.answer: Review and update standards quarterly or when adopting new frameworks. Balance stability with evolution - resist frequent changes but adapt to new best practices in the ecosystem.
